title: Install Hermes locally
summary: Install Hermes, prove the command works from a fresh shell, and leave this lesson with one working local runtime.
objective: Get one clean local Hermes install working end to end.
outcome: A local Hermes CLI that opens cleanly from a fresh shell.
expectedResult: Running `command -v hermes && hermes` from a fresh shell succeeds.
validation: Open a brand-new shell and run `command -v hermes && hermes`. If the command is missing or the CLI errors immediately, this lesson is not done.

steps.title: Install Hermes
steps.body: Run the install command exactly once. Do not compare package managers. Do not branch into extra tooling.
steps.command: curl -fsSL https://hermes.nousresearch.com/install.sh | bash

steps.title: Verify the binary from a fresh shell
steps.body: Close the shell, open a new one, then prove the binary is actually on PATH.
steps.command: command -v hermes

steps.title: Open Hermes and finish one provider setup
steps.body: Launch the CLI and complete exactly one provider path until the CLI is usable. Do not leave this lesson with two half-configured providers.
steps.command: hermes

troubleshooting: If `command -v hermes` returns nothing, restart the shell and check that the install path was added to PATH.
If `hermes` opens and immediately errors on missing credentials, finish one provider setup before moving on.
If the install script fails on a locked-down machine, switch to a user-local install path and rerun the verification step.

title: Run your first agent
summary: Run one real prompt, get one visible answer, and save the transcript as proof.
objective: Prove the runtime can answer one bounded prompt with the provider you just configured.
expectedResult: Hermes answers one small prompt and the transcript is saved in a file you can reopen.
validation: Run one bounded prompt and save both the prompt and answer in a file such as `~/pico-first-run.txt`.

steps.title: Start Hermes from a clean shell
steps.body: Launch the runtime from a new shell so you know you are testing the real install.
steps.command: hermes

steps.title: Send one bounded prompt
steps.body: Use a tiny prompt with an obvious answer. Do not test creativity. Test that the runtime works.
steps.command: Prompt: Give me exactly 3 next steps to test this runtime locally.

steps.title: Save the transcript
steps.body: Write the prompt and the answer into one file so the artifact survives after the terminal closes.
steps.command:
printf "Prompt: Give me exactly 3 next steps to test this runtime locally.
Answer: <paste the actual answer here>
" > ~/pico-first-run.txt

troubleshooting: If the answer times out, switch to a faster model first and rerun the same bounded prompt.
If the answer is nonsense, confirm which provider and model Hermes is actually using before changing the prompt.
If the CLI will not start, go back to the install lesson. This lesson depends on a working binary.

title: Deploy Hermes on a VPS
summary: Put Hermes on an always-on host and prove the remote runtime opens there too.
objective: Move the runtime from your laptop to one persistent machine you control.
outcome: A remote box where Hermes opens cleanly over SSH.
expectedResult: SSH into the host and run `hermes` successfully.
validation: From your machine SSH into the host and run `command -v hermes && hermes`. If that fails, the deployment is not done.

steps.title: Reach the host over SSH
steps.body: Use one box you control and prove SSH works before you touch Hermes.
steps.command: ssh user@your-box

steps.title: Install Hermes on the host
steps.body: Run the same install path on the remote box. Keep the environment boring.
steps.command: curl -fsSL https://hermes.nousresearch.com/install.sh | bash

steps.title: Verify the remote runtime
steps.body: From the SSH session prove the binary exists and the CLI opens.
steps.command: command -v hermes && hermes

troubleshooting: If SSH is unstable, fix the host first. Hermes is not the problem yet.
If the install works locally but not remotely, compare PATH and environment variables before changing commands.
If the remote host is missing provider credentials, add only the minimum config needed for the same provider you already proved locally.

title: Keep your agent alive between sessions
summary: Create one real service definition so logout does not kill the runtime.
objective: Turn the remote runtime into a process that survives disconnects and reboots.
outcome: A saved service definition or tmux command that keeps Hermes alive.
expectedResult: You can disconnect, reconnect, and find the runtime still running.
validation: Start the service, disconnect, reconnect, and verify the Hermes process still exists.

steps.title: Write the systemd unit file
steps.body: Create the service file now. If no file exists at the end of the lesson, you shipped nothing.
steps.command: sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/hermes.service
steps.note:
[Unit]
Description=Hermes Runtime
After=network.target

[Service]
User=<your-user>
WorkingDirectory=/home/<your-user>
ExecStart=/home/<your-user>/.local/bin/hermes
Restart=always
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

steps.title: Enable and start the service
steps.body: Reload systemd, enable the unit, and start it once.
steps.command: sudo systemctl daemon-reload && sudo systemctl enable --now hermes

steps.title: Prove it survives without your shell
steps.body: Check status, disconnect, reconnect, then check again.
steps.command: sudo systemctl status hermes --no-pager

troubleshooting: If the unit fails under systemd but works manually, compare PATH, WorkingDirectory, and provider env vars.
If the service restarts forever, fix the Hermes command first instead of increasing restart counts.
If you cannot use systemd, use one named tmux session and document the exact session name before moving on.

title: Connect a messaging layer
summary: Give the runtime one real ingress path so you can reach it without SSH.
objective: Attach one real inbound channel to the running agent.
outcome: One tested ingress path into the runtime.
expectedResult: A real input reaches the runtime from the chosen channel and produces one visible reply.
validation: Send one real test input through the chosen ingress and record the exact reply.

steps.title: Pick the one channel you already have credentials for
steps.body: Use one ingress only. The goal is a working input path, not channel coverage.
steps.command: Example surfaces: Telegram bot token, Discord bot token, Slack app token, or one webhook endpoint.

steps.title: Store the minimum credentials on the runtime host
steps.body: Put only the required secrets on the box and keep them out of chat logs.
steps.command: export CHANNEL_TOKEN=<your-token>

steps.title: Send one real inbound test
steps.body: Trigger the ingress once and verify the runtime replies exactly once in the expected place.
steps.command: Send one short real message through the selected channel, then record the reply text.

troubleshooting: If the channel is silent, verify token validity and webhook reachability before changing agent logic.
If you get duplicate replies, stop the duplicate runtime or duplicate webhook before continuing.
If the channel reaches the runtime but no reply returns, inspect the running service logs first.

title: Add your first skill/tool
summary: Install one narrow capability the agent can reuse without human hand-holding.
objective: Teach Hermes one bounded task it can perform on demand.
outcome: One working skill or tool the agent can call repeatedly.
expectedResult: The agent uses the skill once on real input and returns a useful result.
validation: Trigger the skill once and save the exact input and output it produced.

steps.title: Pick one narrow job
steps.body: Choose one repeated task that is low-risk and obvious when it works.
steps.command: Example jobs: summarize one note, classify one lead, extract one field set, or rewrite one reply.

steps.title: Install or create the skill
steps.body: Use the existing Hermes skill flow and keep the scope brutally small.
steps.command: Use the single Hermes skill path you already trust. Do not bundle multiple skills into this lesson.

steps.title: Run the skill on real-shaped input
steps.body: Call the skill with input that looks like production, then save the result.
steps.command: Run the skill once and write the resulting artifact to ~/pico-first-skill.txt

troubleshooting: If the skill fails, test the underlying command or API outside Hermes first.
If the output is noisy, cut scope from the skill instead of adding more prompt text.
If the task can send or mutate something risky, keep the skill read-only until the approval lesson.

title: Create a scheduled workflow
summary: Put the useful job on a clock and prove one scheduled execution leaves a visible trace.
objective: Turn the working skill into one repeatable scheduled job.
outcome: One saved schedule that runs the job without manual kickoff.
expectedResult: A scheduled execution fires and leaves a visible result you can point to later.
validation: Trigger one schedule tick and capture the resulting log, output file, or run record.

steps.title: Pick one sane cadence
steps.body: Choose a schedule that matches the job. Daily is enough unless you can defend something tighter.
steps.command: Example cron: 0 9 * * *

steps.title: Save the scheduled command
steps.body: Create the exact cron or scheduler entry now. A schedule that only exists in your head is fake.
steps.command: (crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo "0 9 * * * /home/<your-user>/.local/bin/hermes") | crontab -

steps.title: Force one execution and capture the result
steps.body: Do not wait all day. Trigger the workflow once and save the output location.
steps.command: /home/<your-user>/.local/bin/hermes

troubleshooting: If the schedule never fires, verify timezone and crontab ownership before touching Hermes.
If the job fires twice, remove the duplicate scheduler entry before continuing.
If the scheduler cannot see secrets, export them in the execution environment explicitly.

title: See your agent activity
summary: Open the control surface, inspect one real run or empty-state truth, and record the first operator signal.
objective: Leave the control layer with one concrete signal you can name later.
outcome: One recorded operator signal: a run id, an alert type, or an honest empty-state fact.
expectedResult: You can point to a concrete runtime signal instead of saying "it seems fine".
validation: Open Autopilot and write down one signal in a file such as `~/pico-operator-checkpoint.txt`.

steps.title: Open Autopilot
steps.body: Go to the control surface and start with runs. Do not browse the whole page yet.
steps.command: Open /pico/autopilot

steps.title: Inspect one signal
steps.body: Capture one run id, alert type, or empty-state fact. One is enough for this lesson.
steps.command: Example artifact line: run_id=<paste>, alert_type=<paste>, or empty_state=no_live_agent

steps.title: Save the checkpoint
steps.body: Store that signal in a file so you can compare later states against something concrete.
steps.command:
printf "checkpoint=<paste your first operator signal here>
" > ~/pico-operator-checkpoint.txt

troubleshooting: If no runs appear, verify the scheduled workflow really executed before blaming the control surface.
If auth blocks the view, sign in first. This lesson depends on the actual control plane.
If the page is empty, record the empty-state truth honestly. Empty is still signal.

title: Set a cost threshold
summary: Store one budget line in the sand and verify it survives reload.
objective: Define one cost threshold you are willing to tolerate before the runtime gets noisy.
outcome: One saved threshold tied to the control surface.
expectedResult: The saved threshold persists after reload and appears in the product state.
validation: Save a threshold, reload the page, and confirm the same value is still there.

steps.title: Choose one threshold value
steps.body: Pick one number you can defend right now. Use 75 if you do not have a better reason.
steps.command: Threshold: 75

steps.title: Save it in Autopilot
steps.body: Enter the number and save it once. Do not keep tweaking it during the lesson.
steps.command: Open /pico/autopilot and click Save threshold

steps.title: Reload and verify persistence
steps.body: Reload the page and confirm the same threshold still appears.
steps.command: Reload /pico/autopilot and verify the threshold field still shows your saved value.

troubleshooting: If the threshold disappears after reload, fix persistence before trusting any cost control.
If no live budget exists yet, still save the threshold but treat cost awareness as incomplete.
If you are guessing wildly, choose a lower threshold and move on.

title: Add an approval gate
summary: Create one real approval checkpoint and resolve it on purpose.
objective: Exercise the risky-action gate before you trust the runtime with anything consequential.
outcome: One working approval request and one deliberate resolution.
expectedResult: A pending approval exists and you can approve or reject it intentionally.
validation: Create one approval request and resolve it from the queue.

steps.title: Pick one risky action worth gating
steps.body: Use one outbound send, publish, or state-changing action. If the action is harmless, this lesson is fake.
steps.command: Example gated action: outbound_message_send

steps.title: Create the approval request
steps.body: Push the action into the approval queue instead of running it immediately.
steps.command: Open /pico/autopilot and create one approval request for the risky action.

steps.title: Resolve the request intentionally
steps.body: Approve or reject the request once and record what the queue shows after the action.
steps.command: Use the Approve or Reject action in /pico/autopilot and record the final status.

troubleshooting: If approvals are unavailable, say so plainly. The runtime is not governed yet.
If everybody can approve everything, the gate exists but the policy is weak. Record that gap and keep going.
If you gate trivial noise, the queue becomes spam and operators stop trusting it.

title: Build a lead-response agent
summary: Turn one inbound lead into one draft reply and keep the send behind approval.
objective: Ship one workflow a founder would actually use this week.
outcome: A lead-response workflow with a gated outbound step.
expectedResult: Lead data becomes a draft reply or structured operator summary, and the risky send stays gated.
validation: Run one lead through the workflow end to end and save the drafted result.

steps.title: Create one lead payload
steps.body: Use the smallest useful shape and keep it fixed for the whole lesson.
steps.command:
printf "name=Alex
source=website
intent=book demo
next_step=send reply
" > ~/pico-lead.txt

steps.title: Generate one draft reply
steps.body: Run the workflow on that lead payload and produce one draft reply or one structured summary.
steps.command: Feed ~/pico-lead.txt into your Hermes lead workflow and save the result to ~/pico-lead-draft.txt

steps.title: Put the outbound action behind approval
steps.body: Do not send automatically. Force the workflow through the approval queue once.
steps.command: Create one approval request for the outbound send using the drafted reply as payload.

troubleshooting: If the draft is generic, tighten the lead payload before touching the prompt.
If the workflow is slow, remove extra steps before you optimize models.
If you do not need outbound send yet, stop at a gated draft and call that the artifact.

title: Build a document-processing agent
summary: Ingest one document, extract the few fields that matter, and surface one reviewed output artifact.
objective: Ship one stronger production pattern around unstructured input.
outcome: One document-processing workflow with a saved structured output.
expectedResult: A real document becomes a structured summary, checklist, or tagged record you can inspect.
validation: Process one real document and save the extracted output artifact.

steps.title: Pick one document and freeze the schema
steps.body: Use one real file type and decide the exact fields you need before extraction starts.
steps.command: Example output schema: title, owner, due_date, summary, next_action

steps.title: Run extraction once
steps.body: Extract only the fields that matter downstream. Ignore every nice-to-have field.
steps.command: Run the workflow on one real document and save the structured output to ~/pico-document-output.txt

steps.title: Review the saved output
steps.body: Read the output and confirm it is usable without more cleanup.
steps.command: cat ~/pico-document-output.txt

troubleshooting: If extraction quality is weak, reduce document variety before adding more prompting.
If the output is hard to inspect, your schema is too wide. Cut fields.
If you cannot tell whether the result is usable, the workflow is not ready to automate further.
